segments.title: Writing an alternate history around a Washington Treaty cancellation
segments.summary: The competition asks writers to save a ship or class cancelled around the Washington Naval Treaty and trace the consequences into World War II.
segments.body: The exercise begins with a plausible reason for a warship or class cancelled around the Washington Naval Treaty to survive. Writers must carry that divergence through the 1920s and 1930s, later treaty negotiations, possible refits, and service in World War II. That span forces connections among design, procurement, diplomacy, and operations rather than treating the saved ship as an isolated novelty. The ship's survival must have consequences for its own navy and for the historical setting around it. Opening the video here gives the complete counterfactual premise and the chronological questions an entry must answer.

evidence.note: The transcript states the cancelled-ship premise and the required historical span.

segments.title: Keeping naval alternate history close to real constraints
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke argues that a convincing counterfactual should remain close to real history and account for the many forces shaping warship development.
segments.body: A changed shipbuilding decision cannot float free of diplomacy, technology, and other naval programs. The concentration of cancelled or suspended carriers, cruisers, battleships, and battlecruisers around the Washington Naval Treaty makes the period a particularly rich branching point. Plausibility comes from changing one defensible decision while preserving the surrounding pressures that real navies faced. The result should explain consequences rather than use the divergence as permission for an unrestricted flight of fancy. This watch point gives a compact method for writing naval counterfactuals that still behave like history.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke sets out his close-to-real-history criterion and describes the treaty era as a nexus point.

segments.title: If the Great Kanto earthquake had not doomed Amagi
segments.summary: One example asks how Japanese carrier development changes if earthquake damage does not remove Amagi and Japan completes Amagi alongside Akagi.
segments.body: The proposed divergence removes the earthquake damage that took Amagi out of Japan's carrier-conversion program. Japan would therefore receive Amagi and Akagi as carriers instead of the historical Akagi and Kaga pairing. This is a tightly bounded change because the ships and treaty framework already exist; the writer then has to trace how a different pair affects Japanese carrier development. It is also a useful example of correcting ship-name confusion while keeping the historical question clear. The passage shows how a single external event can redirect a navy's force structure without requiring the entire treaty system to disappear.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes the no-earthquake scenario and clarifies the Amagi and Akagi pairing.

segments.title: Balancing historical reasoning with naval storytelling
segments.summary: The judging framework rewards both technically strong historical integration and a story capable of making the chosen ship's career compelling.
segments.body: The entry has to weave real history into a coherent account of how the surviving ship or class changes events. The top ten would be discussed with selected passages read aloud, while full publication remained at each writer's discretion so a promising idea could still be developed elsewhere. Two prizes recognize that technical historical integration and emotionally effective storytelling are related but distinct achievements. A rigorous paper can excel by tracing consequences carefully, while a more narrative entry can make the ship's altered career compelling. This section is useful for understanding how naval-history writing can balance evidence, causation, and story without reducing any one of them to the only criterion.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the two awards and the balance between historical skill and story.

segments.title: Why the scenario needs 3,000 to 10,000 words
segments.summary: The word limits are designed to provide enough room for historical causation while preventing book-length submissions from overwhelming shorter entries.
segments.body: A very short paper cannot adequately connect ship design, treaty politics, interwar service, and wartime consequences. The 3,000-word minimum sets a realistic expectation for developing that causal chain. The 10,000-word ceiling prevents a book-length submission from overwhelming shorter entries through sheer volume and keeps judging more comparable. Dr. Clarke tested the scale with his own outline and estimated that a history-heavy version could work at roughly 6,500 words. The limits therefore encourage enough depth to explain consequences while still forcing the writer to select the most important developments.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains the minimum, upper limit, fairness concern, and his own test outline.

segments.title: Carrying the counterfactual into a World War II test
segments.summary: Entries should span the post-World War I settlement into World War II and show how the surviving ship performs when war complicates the scenario.
segments.body: The scenario must run from the end of World War I to some point in World War II rather than ending with an interwar design decision. Dr. Clarke prefers the account to reach at least 1940 so the writer can show the ship in action or explain what wartime events it would encounter. He also cautions that war rapidly multiplies interactions among ships, units, and campaigns, making later consequences harder to contain. That complexity is part of the historical test: the saved ship must perform inside a changing conflict, not simply exist in a fleet list. This watch point clarifies why the assignment's chronology is designed to reveal operational as well as procurement consequences.

evidence.note: The transcript requires a span from the end of World War I into World War II and explains why wartime interactions complicate the scenario.

segments.title: Choosing the F3 design instead of Nelson and Rodney
segments.summary: The F3 design illustrates how a treaty-compliant choice could alter Royal Navy speed, armament, later gun upgrades, and interwar deployments.
segments.body: Because the F3 concept could fit within the treaty framework, the divergence can come from selecting a different British design rather than rewriting the agreement itself. Dr. Clarke contrasts it with Nelson and Rodney and highlights roughly 28-knot performance with 15-inch/50-calibre guns. Choosing F3 would then raise broader questions about whether the Royal Navy standardizes that longer gun across its 15-inch battleships and how later King George V-class choices develop. The ships' greater speed would also change where they could deploy and what operations they could undertake during the interwar years. The example demonstrates how a treaty-compliant procurement choice can produce technical, doctrinal, and operational consequences far beyond two hulls.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts F3 with Nelson and Rodney and traces armament, speed, and deployment implications.

segments.title: Could an Admiral-class hull have become an early carrier?
segments.summary: A carefully qualified hypothesis links Cammell Laird's later carrier expertise to possible studies for converting an Admiral-class battlecruiser.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke observes that Cammell Laird appeared unusually capable in large strike-carrier design by the time of Ark Royal despite lacking an obvious earlier carrier build. He suspects that the yard may have studied converting an Admiral-class battlecruiser into a carrier, but he has not found the documentary proof needed to state that conclusion in a history book. The timing of any study matters because work could have begun when the class was paused, when cancellation became likely, or even earlier. An earlier decision could put an Admiral-class carrier into service before the Washington Naval Treaty and change the position from which Britain negotiated. The passage is especially valuable as a demonstration of how historians can present a reasoned archival hypothesis while clearly separating suspicion from established fact.

evidence.note: The transcript presents and explicitly qualifies the Cammell Laird and Admiral-class conversion hypothesis.

segments.title: Why a treaty-era capital ship could not remain secret
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke rejects hidden battleship scenarios because reconnaissance, visible shipyards, industrial logistics, and large workforces make concealment implausible.
segments.body: Cruiser aircraft and naval patrols created many opportunities to discover activity at remote Pacific bases, especially when treaty restrictions made powers alert to unauthorized development. Shooting at an overflying aircraft or an investigating cruiser would reveal the secret and might start a war before the hidden ship was ready. Established shipyards were also observable by naval attaches, leaving no easy place for a major capital ship to disappear. Constructing a secret yard would require conspicuous machinery, skilled workers, transport, and a supporting population. The industrial and human footprint therefore defeats the premise even before the warship enters service, making secret-battleship scenarios a poor foundation for a close-to-history counterfactual.

evidence.note: The transcript details reconnaissance, diplomatic observation, and industrial reasons a warship cannot be hidden.

segments.title: How Admiral-class carriers would reshape treaty tonnage
segments.summary: Three British Admiral-class carriers would invite an American response and force every treaty navy to reconsider carrier tonnage and conversion choices.
segments.body: Three large British Admiral-class carriers completed before the treaty would be politically difficult for the Royal Navy to surrender. Their existence could push the United States toward three Lexington-class conversions in order to match British strength. That response would alter the carrier tonnage available to Japan, France, and Italy, so the divergence spreads through the entire treaty system. Britain would also have to decide whether it still needs to convert Courageous and Glorious when it already possesses a powerful carrier force. The example shows why saving one class can reshape multinational force planning rather than merely add three ships to a British list.

evidence.note: The transcript traces British carrier retention through US matching and multinational tonnage effects.

segments.title: Following other navies' reactions to one changed program
segments.summary: A credible scenario must acknowledge how rival navies respond, illustrated by completing every Colorado and reconsidering the number and design of British Nelsons.
segments.body: Writers need not provide complete parallel histories for every foreign design, but they should show how other powers react where those choices touch the main story. If the United States completes every Colorado-class battleship, Britain and Japan cannot be assumed to proceed unchanged. Britain might build three Nelsons instead of two or alter their balance of speed and survivability because a larger class changes the value placed on each individual hull. Those responses would feed back into treaty tonnage and the competitive position of the original ship. The passage offers a practical boundary for counterfactual research: follow foreign reactions far enough to explain their contact with the central story, without writing an exhaustive history of every navy.

evidence.note: The transcript explains reaction chains using completed Colorados and possible British Nelson responses.

segments.title: Ark Royal and the first purpose-designed carrier
segments.summary: The closing preview identifies the early Ark Royal as the first ship designed from the outset as a carrier while distinguishing its seaplane role from later flight-deck carriers.
segments.body: The closing preview points to the earliest Ark Royal as the first ship designed from the outset for carrier service rather than converted from another warship or merchant hull. Dr. Clarke immediately qualifies the claim by identifying it as a seaplane carrier, not an aircraft carrier in the later flight-deck sense. That distinction matters because purpose-built naval aviation began before the familiar form of the fleet carrier was established. Opening the video here gives a concise bridge from the competition's conversion counterfactuals to the real design history of specialized carrier ships.

evidence.note: The transcript previews the early Ark Royal and distinguishes the first purpose-designed seaplane carrier from a later aircraft carrier.
